preFix.title: From Tablet to Codex
preFix.subtitle: How the modern book form came to replace scrolls and wax tablets in the ancient world
preFix.excerpt: The codex, the form that underlies every book in use today, displaced the papyrus scroll and the wax writing tablet over the first four centuries of the Common Era. This reading traces that transition and the material reasons behind it.
preFix.sourceNotes: Historical account based on Davenport, Cyril. The Book: Its History and Development. London: Constable, 1907. Coverage of tablet and scroll antecedents and the emergence of the codex form.

content.text: Before the book: tablets and scrolls

The oldest writing surfaces in long use were clay tablets. People across Mesopotamia used them from at least the fourth millennium BCE. Clay is cheap, durable once fired, and could be pressed with a stylus before it dried. Tens of thousands survive, covering records, contracts, and literary texts including the Epic of Gilgamesh. Their drawback is plain: a clay library is very heavy and cannot be changed without destroying the record.

Greek and Roman writers used wax tablets routinely for everyday notes, accounts, and letters. A wooden frame filled with recessed wax was written on with a pointed stylus; the blunt end of the stylus smoothed the wax to erase. Wax tablets were reusable by design, equivalent in function to a modern erasable notepad. Several tablets could be lashed together along one edge to form a polyptych, a hinge arrangement that is the direct structural ancestor of the
codex

The papyrus scroll dominated literary and administrative culture throughout the classical Mediterranean world. Papyrus was made from the pith of the Cyperus papyrus plant, pressed into sheets and glued together in a long horizontal roll. A substantial text might fill a roll 10 metres long. Reading a scroll required both hands: one to unroll, one to roll up the portion already read. Finding a passage meant unrolling to the right place, which made random access laborious.
Parchment and the conditions for change

Parchment, made from the stretched and dried skin of a sheep, goat, or calf, could be written on both sides, which papyrus could not reliably manage. It was more durable in northern climates and could be folded without cracking. The ancient writer Pliny the Elder attributed the invention of parchment to the city of Pergamon in the second century BCE, though the material was certainly older. The name parchment is a corruption of the Latin pergamena, from Pergamon.

Parchment made the codex form practical in a way that papyrus had not. Papyrus, with the grain running horizontally in the sheet, cracks when folded repeatedly. A parchment sheet folds cleanly and holds the fold. Gatherings of folded parchment could be nested inside each other, forming a
quire
, and multiple quires sewn together formed a book that could be opened to any page directly.
The rise of the codex

The earliest surviving parchment codices date from the first and second centuries CE. Martial, writing around 85 CE, mentions small parchment codices of his own works that a traveller could carry conveniently on a journey, as compared with a cumbersome scroll. The codex format was adopted with particular speed by early Christian communities, who used it for copying the texts that would become the New Testament. By the fourth century, the great scriptoria of the Christian world worked almost exclusively in the codex form.

The practical advantages were decisive. A codex holds more text per unit of material than a scroll, because both sides of every page are used. It is easier to navigate, because any page can be reached directly. It is more robust in regular use: the spine and boards protect the text pages. And unlike a wax tablet, it is permanent.
Reuse and the palimpsest
Parchment was expensive. In periods of scarcity or poverty, older manuscripts were scraped clean and rewritten. A manuscript reused in this way is a
palimpsest

. The original text was never fully removed: the ink penetrated the surface layer of the skin and traces remained legible under the later writing. Modern imaging techniques, particularly multispectral photography and X-ray fluorescence, have recovered texts from palimpsests that were unknown for centuries.

The best-known example is the Archimedes Palimpsest. It is a thirteenth-century Byzantine prayer book written over a tenth-century copy of mathematical works by Archimedes. By the early twentieth century the Archimedes text was effectively lost. Multispectral imaging in the 1990s and 2000s recovered most of it. This included the only known copy of his work on the method of exhaustion.
Structure of the medieval manuscript codex
The medieval scriptorium organised its work around the
quire

. A scribe would work through a quire of typically 4 folded sheets (a quaternion, giving 16 pages) before passing it to the next workstation. Each quire was numbered, so that the gatherings could be collated in the correct order for sewing. This practice is directly continued in modern hand bookbinding, where each gathering is still marked at the foot of the first page for collation.

The boards of medieval bindings were real wooden boards, often oak or beech, up to 10 mm thick for large liturgical volumes. They were attached by lacing the sewing supports through channels cut into the board edge, a technique that produces a far more robust hinge than the simple case binding in use today. The boards protected the text pages and, when the book was stored flat on a shelf, provided the pressure that kept the pages in good condition.
From manuscript to print

Gutenberg's press in the 1450s changed the economy of the book but not its physical form. The earliest printed books, the incunabula produced before 1501, were set in type designed to mimic manuscript hands. They were printed on the same parchment and paper stocks and bound in the same wooden-board structures as manuscripts. The printed book inherited the codex form without question, because no competing form offered the same combination of navigability, density, and durability.

The paper codex, sewn on tapes or cords and covered in leather or cloth, remained the dominant form of information storage for four centuries after Gutenberg. The basic structure has changed less in five hundred years than almost any other technology in continuous use.
